module: Sepsis and Septic Shock Part II: Resuscitation and hemodynamic support of the patient with sepsis

questions.question: Result of which clinical trial best supports the use of balanced solution compared to normal saline in reducing renal injury in case of septic shock?
questions.explanation: The SMART trial (Isotonic Solutions and Major Adverse Renal Events Trial) was a clinical trial planned to determine the effect of isotonic crystalloid composition on clinical outcomes for critically ill adults. It found lower rates of acute kidney injury, renal replacement therapy, or death with balanced crystalloids versus normal saline. Intravenous saline may be associated with hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, AKI and mortality. SALT-ED, BASIC, SPLIT, and SALT are separate trials examining similar questions but SMART best supports balanced solutions for renal protection in septic shock.
questions.topic: SMART trial balanced crystalloids saline renal injury septic shock

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a potential therapy for the treatment of refractory septic shock?
questions.explanation: Phentolamine is a nonselective alpha-adrenergic antagonist used for hypertension, hypertensive emergencies, pheochromocytoma, vasospasm of Raynaud disease, clonidine withdrawal, and prevention of skin injury from extravasated norepinephrine. It is not used in refractory septic shock. Angiotensin II (ATHOS-3), methylene blue (NO-cGMP inhibitor), vasopressin, hydrocortisone +/- fludrocortisone, and ECMO have all been proposed as treatment options for refractory septic shock, though data for their use are admittedly limited.
questions.topic: refractory septic shock phentolamine vasopressors angiotensin methylene blue ECMO

questions.question: Which of the following drugs is the first-line recommended vasopressor for the treatment of septic shock?
questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is the first-choice vasopressor for septic shock per SSC guidelines (strong recommendation, moderate quality of evidence). Dopamine is an alternative only in highly selected patients with bradycardia and low risk of tachyarrhythmia. Vasopressin and epinephrine are adjunctive agents to norepinephrine — to decrease high norepinephrine doses or raise MAP if norepinephrine alone is insufficient. Phenylephrine is generally discouraged due to lack of evidence of clinical utility in sepsis.
questions.topic: norepinephrine vasopressor first-line septic shock SSC

questions.question: Which best supports the diagnosis of hypoperfusion?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Elevated lactate and elevated ScVO2

statements.label: B
statements.text: Elevated lactate and decreased ScVO2

statements.label: C
statements.text: Normal lactate and decreased ScVO2

statements.label: D
statements.text: Normal lactate and normal ScVO2

questions.explanation: Serum lactate >2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion associated with organ dysfunction in critically ill patients; in tissue hypoxia, lactate is overproduced by increased anaerobic glycolysis. ScvO2 is a surrogate for SvO2 reflecting the balance between oxygen delivery (DO2) and consumption (VO2). Normal SvO2 range is 60–80%. ScvO2 and SvO2 are usually below normal in hypovolaemia and cardiogenic shock (low-flow states) but are usually high in distributive shock such as septic shock. Elevated lactate combined with decreased ScvO2 best supports hypoperfusion.
questions.topic: hypoperfusion lactate ScvO2 SvO2 oxygen delivery septic shock

questions.question: According to the Surviving Sepsis Campaign, which of the following should be done within the first hour:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Measure lactate level. Re-measure if initial lactate is >4 mmol/L

statements.label: B
statements.text: Obtain blood cultures prior to administration of antibiotics

statements.label: C
statements.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics

statements.label: D
statements.text: Administer rapid 30 ml/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥2 mmol/L

questions.explanation: The SSC hour-1 bundle includes: measure lactate (re-measure if initial lactate >2 mmol/L, not >4), obtain blood cultures prior to antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, rapidly administer 30 ml/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L (not ≥2), and apply vasopressors if hypotensive during or after fluid resuscitation to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg. Statement A is false because re-measure threshold is >2 mmol/L. Statement D is false because the fluid bolus threshold is lactate ≥4 mmol/L.
questions.topic: SSC hour-1 bundle lactate blood cultures antibiotics fluid resuscitation

questions.question: Regarding vasopressors that can be used in sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Norepinephrine is a potent α-1 and β-1 adrenergic agonist

statements.label: B
statements.text: Dopamine acts in a dose-dependent manner on D-1, α-1 and β-2 adrenergic receptors

statements.label: C
statements.text: Selepressin is a highly selective V1 agonist

statements.label: D
statements.text: Vasopressin acts through V1 receptors

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine acts on α-1 and β-1 receptors (increases venous and arterial tone, preload, and contractility). Dopamine acts dose-dependently on D-1, D-2, α-1, and β-1 receptors — NOT β-2; this statement is false. Selepressin is a highly selective V1a agonist (unlike vasopressin which acts on V1, V2, and V1b receptors). Vasopressin primarily acts through V1 (vascular smooth muscle), V2 (water retention), and V1b receptors.
questions.topic: vasopressors norepinephrine dopamine vasopressin selepressin receptors

questions.question: Regarding the choice of fluids in sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Normal saline is the fluid of choice

statements.label: B
statements.text: Crystalloids remain first-line fluids for resuscitation in sepsis

statements.label: C
statements.text: In patients who fail to respond to crystalloids, starches can be administered as a second line of fluid resuscitation

statements.label: D
statements.text: Gelatins should not be used for fluid resuscitation in sepsis and septic shock

questions.explanation: Crystalloids (not normal saline specifically) remain first-line for resuscitation — balanced crystalloids are preferred over normal saline due to risk of hyperchloremic acidosis with saline. Hydroxyethyl starches (HES) are specifically recommended against by the SSC due to associations with organ injury and increased mortality — they should NOT be used as second-line. Current evidence no longer supports superiority of colloids over crystalloids. Gelatins are also not recommended for resuscitation in sepsis and septic shock.
questions.topic: fluids crystalloids starches gelatins normal saline balanced crystalloids sepsis

questions.question: Which of the following modalities does NOT have robust evidence to support its use in guiding intravenous fluid resuscitation efforts in sepsis?
options: C: Variation in inferior vena cava diameter by Ultrasonography
questions.explanation: Mini-fluid challenges (small boluses of fluid, 50-100 mL) coupled with reassessments of hemodynamic variables, while intuitively appealing, have not yet been rigorously studied in a randomised controlled trial and thus lack robust evidence. Passive leg raising, IVC diameter variation by ultrasonography, pulse pressure variation, and end-expiratory occlusion manoeuvre all have supportive clinical trial evidence to support their application in sepsis resuscitation.
questions.topic: fluid responsiveness mini-fluid challenge PLR IVC PPV EEOM

questions.question: Which of the following are recommended in the early phases of septic shock resuscitation?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Dopamine is the first-choice vasopressor in a patient with a reduced ejection fraction

statements.label: B
statements.text: Fluid resuscitation and vasopressor support (if needed) target a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 65 mmHg

statements.label: C
statements.text: After initial fluid resuscitation, additional fluid administration is recommended to be guided by dynamic assessments of hemodynamic status

statements.label: D
statements.text: Initial fixed fluid bolus of 40 mL/kg to be given within the first 3 hours

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine (not dopamine) is first-choice vasopressor; dopamine is reserved for patients with bradycardia and low tachyarrhythmia risk. MAP target is 65 mmHg per SSC guidelines. Dynamic measures are recommended over physical examination or static parameters alone to guide ongoing fluid resuscitation. The recommended initial fluid bolus is at least 30 mL/kg (not 40 mL/kg) within the first 3 hours of resuscitation.
questions.topic: septic shock resuscitation MAP target norepinephrine dynamic measures fluid bolus 30ml/kg

questions.question: Regarding initial fluid resuscitation in sepsis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Fluid resuscitation should be initiated immediately

statements.label: B
statements.text: At least 30 mL/kg should be administered within the first 24 hours

statements.label: C
statements.text: Dynamic measures to guide fluid resuscitation are recommended over clinical examination

statements.label: D
statements.text: Central venous pressure and central venous oxygen saturation are the parameters of choice

questions.explanation: Fluid resuscitation should be initiated immediately in sepsis-induced tissue hypoperfusion. The SSC recommends at least 30 mL/kg of IV crystalloid within the first 3 hours (not 24 hours). Dynamic measures are recommended over physical examination or static parameters alone. CVP and ScvO2 are NOT the parameters of choice — dynamic measures (PLR, PPV, IVC variation, EEOM) are preferred; static parameters like CVP poorly predict fluid responsiveness.
questions.topic: fluid resuscitation 30ml/kg dynamic measures CVP static parameters sepsis

questions.question: A 20 year old male who is febrile and having body ache and headache since 3 days. On arrival in emergency dept. of hospital he was found to be febrile (102.9°F/39.4°C), tachycardic (130/minute), tachypnoeic (35/min) and hypotensive (80/40 mmHg). Chest auscultation revealed B/L crepts. Patient was given 1 litre of RL bolus. What would be best method for predicting cardiac output in response to fluid administration?
questions.explanation: Passive leg raising (PLR) is the best method for predicting fluid responsiveness in this context. PLR modifies preload in various ways without fluid loading and can be performed at the bedside in the ED with the help of staff and in spontaneously breathing patients. It is non-invasive, cheap, and has very good predictive value for fluid responsiveness. Fluid challenge itself cannot be repeated as it is harmful if repeated cardiac output assessment is required. PPV and SVCV require controlled mechanical ventilation. IVC variation is less reliable in spontaneously breathing patients.
questions.topic: passive leg raising fluid responsiveness cardiac output spontaneous breathing

questions.question: Maximum dose of infusion of norepinephrine in septic shock is:
questions.explanation: The maximum dose of norepinephrine in septic shock is 5 µg/kg/min. The weight-based mean dose (WMD) strongly associated with mortality has a cutoff of 0.75 µg/kg/min, and the weight-based maximum dose (WMax) has a cutoff of 2.30 µg/kg/min. Some studies identified a threshold associated with 100% mortality at 2.22–3.8 µg/kg/min; however, some patients survived with doses >4 µg/kg/min. Maximal dose-effect exists but dose-response curves in humans are unavailable due to ethical concerns. Norepinephrine has a linear effect up to 1.35 mcg/kg/min in rat studies.
questions.topic: norepinephrine maximum dose septic shock vasopressor titration

questions.question: Which of the following IV fluids are NOT recommended for use in sepsis resuscitation?
questions.explanation: Hydroxyethyl starch (HES) is specifically recommended against by the SSC due to associations with organ injury and increased mortality. Crystalloids (Plasmalyte, normal saline, Lactated Ringers) are recommended as first-line fluids for initial resuscitation — balanced crystalloids (Plasmalyte, LR) are preferred over normal saline. Albumin can be considered in addition to crystalloids, particularly in patients requiring substantial amounts of crystalloids.
questions.topic: HES hydroxyethyl starch fluid resuscitation SSC albumin crystalloids

questions.question: Goals of initial resuscitation according to the Surviving Sepsis guidelines for patients with sepsis-induced tissue hypoperfusion:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Central venous pressure (CVP) 8-12 mmHg

statements.label: B
statements.text: Mean arterial pressure (MAP) >65 mmHg

statements.label: C
statements.text: Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/h

statements.label: D
statements.text: Central venous (SVC) saturations of 70%

questions.explanation: The primary initial goal per current SSC guidelines is MAP >65 mmHg achieved by fluids and subsequently vasopressors. CVP 8-12 mmHg, urine output >0.5 ml/kg/h, and ScvO2 >70% (or mixed venous saturation >65%) are later targets during the first 6 hours from older SSC guidelines (2012 early goal-directed therapy targets). Current guidelines have moved away from these static targets and instead recommend dynamic assessment of haemodynamic status. MAP >65 mmHg remains the primary goal.
questions.topic: SSC resuscitation goals MAP CVP urine output ScvO2 EGDT
